name: Dune — Arrakis, the Imperium & the Atreides

entries.name: Arrakis — The Desert Planet
entries.content: Arrakis — known in the Imperium simply as Dune — is the third planet of Canopus and the most important world in the known universe. It is the sole source of the spice melange. The planet is almost entirely desert: a vast, continent-spanning expanse of ergs, regs, rock formations, and polar regions — but no open water. The surface is brutal to everything not adapted to it. Daytime temperatures in the deep desert reach 70°C and above. The prevailing winds carry sand that abrades exposed flesh and clogs unprotected machinery. The sandworms — Shai-Hulud — make open desert travel effectively impossible without Fremen knowledge. The Arrakeen basin, where the ducal city sits, is the most temperate inhabited region and is still a place where every drop of moisture is precious. The sky is a perpetual amber-brown haze filtered through suspended spice particles. The air has a faint sweetness at the edge of every breath: cinnamon and something older, something that comes from deep rock and geological time. Arrakis was habitable once — the polar caps have ice, and the Fremen carry a vision of what the planet could be returned to. The planet is currently an ecological prison, but the Fremen know something that the Imperium does not: the prison has a door.
entries.comment: Core setting — Arrakis itself

entries.name: Spice Melange
entries.content: The spice melange is the most valuable substance in the known universe and is found only on Arrakis. It is produced by sandtrout — immature sandworms — in a biological process that involves water, deep sand chemistry, and the sandworm lifecycle. Melange has several properties that make it essential to Imperial civilisation: it extends human lifespan significantly (decades with regular use; centuries with intensive use), heightens awareness and perception, and, at sufficient concentration, enables the prescience that the Spacing Guild Navigators depend on for safe interstellar travel. Without melange, the Guild cannot fold space and interstellar civilisation effectively ceases. This gives Arrakis a monopoly leverage over the entire Imperium that successive Emperors have managed carefully and with considerable fear. Melange smells of cinnamon and something else — ancient, complex, not entirely pleasant at first. It stains the eyes: regular users develop the blue-within-blue irises called the Eyes of Ibad among the Fremen. It is physically addictive. Withdrawal can be lethal. The Fremen live immersed in it, metabolise it from birth, and develop tolerance and capabilities — including limited prescience — that spice-starved off-worlders lack. Jessica's body has been processing ambient Arrakeen spice since arrival; her eyes have not yet fully turned.
entries.comment: Spice — the central substance, highest priority

entries.name: The Sandworms — Shai-Hulud
entries.content: The sandworms of Arrakis — Shai-Hulud, the Great Maker — are enormous invertebrate creatures that live beneath the deep sand and are integral to both the planet's ecology and the spice cycle. Mature specimens can reach hundreds of meters in length. They are attracted to rhythmic vibration — the thumpers the Fremen use for calling, the irregular walking patterns of those untrained in the desert walk, the plodding of off-world machinery. They are essentially invulnerable to conventional weapons at scale. They are reverent figures to the Fremen — not gods exactly, but something close to an ecological deity, intimately connected to the spice cycle and to the water-of-life, the exhalation of a drowned worm that is the most dangerous and most transformative substance on the planet. The Fremen ride them — a technique of goading the worm to the surface and mounting it, using spreaders to keep the segments open and direct the creature. It requires courage, training, and timing that cannot be faked. A Fremen who cannot ride is not yet fully Fremen.
entries.comment: The sandworms — critical world element

entries.name: The Fremen
entries.content: The Fremen are the indigenous people of Arrakis — descendants of the Zensunni Wanderers, a religious diaspora that arrived on Arrakis generations ago and adapted over centuries to become the most effective desert survivors in the known universe. The Imperium and the Harkonnens have systematically underestimated the Fremen: Imperial census records list a fraction of their actual number, and most off-worlders believe them to be scattered, primitive, and manageable. They are none of these things. The Fremen live in sietches — deep underground communities carved into the rock, thermally sealed, water-efficient, and defended with a ferocity that has destroyed every Harkonnen punitive expedition that has been sent against them. They are elite warriors, trained from childhood. Their combat philosophy involves the use of the weirding way — a term the Fremen use for extraordinary physical capability, which overlaps in interesting ways with the Bene Gesserit prana-bindu system. They carry personal distillation suits — stillsuits — that reclaim virtually all body moisture and allow desert survival indefinitely. They have a long-term terraforming vision for Arrakis, tended by generations of Fremen and reinforced by Bene Gesserit ecological manipulations, that would transform the desert into a garden world. The Bene Gesserit planted a messianic prophecy among them — the Missionaria Protectiva — which has been growing for generations. It describes the arrival of a superbeing and his mother, who will lead the Fremen to liberation. It was designed to protect Bene Gesserit operatives. It is now operating as designed, and possibly as something more.
entries.comment: The Fremen people — high priority

entries.name: The Bene Gesserit
entries.content: The Bene Gesserit Sisterhood is a quasi-religious order of women who have spent millennia pursuing a single goal: the creation of the Kwisatz Haderach — a human being with prescient access to both male and female genetic memory simultaneously, capable of seeing what no Oracle or Navigator can see. Their breeding programme is ninety generations old. They do not rule; they advise, manipulate, and position. Every major house has a Bene Gesserit Truthsayer or Advisor. Their power is influence over others, not formal authority. Bene Gesserit training includes: the Voice (a precise modulation of speech that compels obedience in the undertrained listener); prana-bindu muscle and nerve control (the ability to control the body from the autonomic level, including heartbeat, pain suppression, pheromone production, physical capability that borders on superhuman); the Weirding Way (combat application of prana-bindu, extremely fast and precise); and advanced observation and analysis techniques that function as a kind of organic intelligence apparatus. Reverend Mothers have undergone the Spice Agony — a transformation using the water-of-life that kills most who attempt it, and grants the survivors access to all the female-line genetic memory they carry. Jessica has not yet undergone this. The Sisterhood is not evil but it is not good. It is an institution pursuing a centuries-long plan that treats individual humans, including its own members, as instruments rather than ends. Jessica's disobedience — bearing a son instead of the instructed daughter — is the single most consequential deviation from the programme in living memory.
entries.comment: The Bene Gesserit — extremely high priority

entries.name: Duke Leto Atreides
entries.content: Leto Atreides I is the Duke of House Atreides — head of a Great House of the Landsraad with a lineage tracing back to ancient Agamemnon and a reputation built on personal honour, fair governance, and the loyalty of his people. He is tall, dark, and physically imposing in the way of trained fighters who have learned to hold their capability in reserve. He governs by trust rather than fear, which makes him unusual among the Great Houses and deeply suspicious to the Emperor, who has positioned the Atreides against the Harkonnens on Arrakis specifically because he believes House Atreides is becoming too popular and too powerful. Leto knows Arrakis is a trap. He has accepted it anyway because refusing would have been a different kind of death — the slow death of a House that backed down. He is in love with Jessica in a way that has lasted seventeen years and shows no signs of diminishing, complicated by the fact that he has not married her — the concubine arrangement is a political calculation, leaving him technically available for a political marriage, though he has not made one. He is a man of profound honour in a system that punishes honour, and he is fully aware of this, and he proceeds anyway. Jessica knows he is very likely to die on Arrakis. She has not told him.
entries.comment: Duke Leto — core relationship, highest priority

entries.name: Paul Atreides
entries.content: Paul Atreides is Jessica and Leto's son — approximately fifteen to seventeen at the time of the move to Arrakis. He is the product of the Bene Gesserit breeding programme one generation ahead of schedule, trained by Jessica in the Bene Gesserit way from childhood in addition to the Mentat training and ducal education a lord's son receives. Paul is not ordinary. He has been showing signs of prescient dreaming — flashes of possible futures — since before Arrakis. He has already passed the Reverend Mother Gaius Helen Mohiam's gom jabbar test, which would have killed an ordinary person. He has something in him that neither Jessica nor the Sisterhood fully understands yet. The Fremen prophecy describes him as the Mahdi — the one who will lead them to liberty. The Bene Gesserit fear he is the Kwisatz Haderach, arrived too early, without their controls. Jessica watches him becoming something she cannot entirely follow and feels the pride and the terror in the same breath. Paul's bond with his mother is close and complicated: she gave him capabilities the Sisterhood would have denied him and he knows this, and he also knows she is carrying something she has not told him, and he has inherited enough of her perceptiveness to know that too.
entries.comment: Paul — Jessica's son, the central figure, highest priority

entries.name: The Kwisatz Haderach
entries.content: The Kwisatz Haderach — 'shortening of the way' in Bene Gesserit parlance — is the culminating goal of ninety generations of Bene Gesserit selective breeding: a human being who possesses prescient awareness of both male and female genetic memory, capable of seeing futures and pasts that no Reverend Mother can access (female memory only) and no Guild Navigator can fully map. The Kwisatz Haderach would be, in theory, a perfect instrument for the Sisterhood's purposes — the most capable Oracle ever produced, under their control. Jessica's son may be this being. If he is, he arrived one generation early, without the careful conditioning the Sisterhood planned, and with a mother who trained him herself rather than surrendering him to Sisterhood formation. This means, if he is the Kwisatz Haderach, he is not under their control. The Reverend Mother Mohiam has assessed him and is afraid of what she found. The Bene Gesserit relationship to Paul is therefore complicated: he may be exactly what they worked for, and he may be entirely beyond them.
entries.comment: The Kwisatz Haderach — the central prophecy concept

entries.name: The Harkonnen — House Harkonnen
entries.content: House Harkonnen is the Atreides' hereditary enemy and the former holders of the Arrakis fief. Baron Vladimir Harkonnen is the head of the House — corpulent beyond natural scale (a medical condition he has compensated for with suspensor technology), brilliant in the way of people who have no ethical constraints on their intelligence, and deeply, specifically hateful of House Atreides, whose honour makes his own excess visible by contrast. The Baron administered Arrakis for years, exploiting the spice harvest through slave labour and Fremen extraction, building personal wealth at a scale that challenged the Emperor. He was stripped of Arrakis by Imperial decree and replaced by the Atreides — a political move that he correctly identified as an opportunity rather than a loss, since it placed House Atreides in a position the Harkonnens know intimately and have prepared to destroy. The Baron has a plan for Arrakis. It involves Imperial Sardaukar troops fighting under Harkonnen colours. It is already in motion. Jessica knows this and cannot stop it and cannot tell Leto in a way that would not require explaining knowledge she should not have. She carries this also. What Jessica does not know, or has not fully confronted, is that the Baron is her father — her mother was the Bene Gesserit Reverend Mother Gaius Helen Mohiam, and the Baron fathered Jessica on her as part of a complicated arrangement decades ago. Jessica was never told.
entries.comment: The Harkonnens — the enemy

entries.name: The Padishah Emperor
entries.content: Shaddam IV is the Padishah Emperor — ruler of the Known Universe, theoretically sovereign over the Great Houses, the Guild, and the Bene Gesserit, in practice a political balance maintained by mutual fear and the control of spice. His throne is perpetually threatened by the growing power of the Great Houses, and the Atreides specifically — whose popularity with other Houses approaches a legitimacy that competes with his own. He has conspired with the Harkonnens against House Atreides. This is a violation of Imperial law. He justifies it to himself and his advisors as necessary statecraft; it is also a personal fear of a Duke more loved than an Emperor. He commands the Sardaukar — Imperial military forces of terrifying effectiveness, recruited and hardened on the death-world of Salusa Secundus, who fight as elite troops under Imperial colours or, in the Arrakis operation, disguised as Harkonnen forces. The Emperor's involvement in the Arrakis trap is not public knowledge. Jessica knows it. She cannot prove it in a way that would help.
entries.comment: The Emperor — the political top

entries.name: The Sardaukar
entries.content: The Sardaukar are the Emperor's elite military force — recruits selected and brutalised on Salusa Secundus, a prison world whose conditions kill most of those sent there. Those who survive are already exceptional; they are then trained into a military capability that no Great House force can match in direct engagement. The Sardaukar are the Emperor's most closely held military advantage, and he deploys them at Arrakis disguised as Harkonnen troops, maintaining the fiction of Imperial non-involvement. Their presence changes the tactical calculus entirely: even the Atreides' elite units, including the Fremen-trained Sergeant Kynes' assessors and the household troops, cannot hold against Sardaukar in open engagement. The only forces that might match them are the Fremen themselves, who have been surviving against Harkonnen extraction for generations and have been shaped by the same kind of brutal environment for longer.
entries.comment: The Sardaukar — Imperial elite troops

entries.name: The Stillsuit
entries.content: The stillsuit is the technology that makes the Fremen desert-adapted to an extraordinary degree. It is a full-body garment made of microporous membranes that reclaim virtually all moisture expelled by the body — sweat, breath, urine — through a system of tubes, filters, and sacs, cycling it back as clean drinking water drawn from a tube at the collar. A well-maintained stillsuit allows indefinite desert survival without external water supply, which is what makes Fremen deep-desert travel possible and off-worlders' attempts at the same invariably fatal. Fremen still-suit discipline is extremely precise: the suit must be sealed correctly, the plugs must be worn even while sleeping, the filter loads must be changed on schedule. The moisture wasted by off-worlders in normal activity is, by Fremen standards, criminal — not as a moral judgement, but as a factual inefficiency in an environment where water is the currency of survival. Jessica and Paul receive stillsuits and are taught to wear them. Learning to inhabit the suit is one of the first tests of adaptability for any off-worlder attempting to move in Fremen company.
entries.comment: The stillsuit — critical practical world element

entries.name: The Sietch
entries.content: A sietch is a Fremen community — typically carved into rock formations or deep under the desert surface, sealed against heat and sand, internally maintained at a temperature and humidity level that would be unimaginable in the open desert. A sietch is the basic unit of Fremen social organisation: its members are naib-led (a naib is both a military and civic leader), bound by water covenant, and organised around both the practical requirements of desert survival and a rich ceremonial and social life. Sietches hold the Fremen's accumulated water in catchbasins and cisterns — resources that represent centuries of careful harvesting and represent the long-term terraforming vision. To enter a sietch is to be brought into something that the Fremen protect with their lives. Sietch Tabr, led by the Naib Stilgar, is the sietch Jessica and Paul will eventually reach after the attack on Arrakeen.
entries.comment: The sietch — Fremen community structure

entries.name: Stilgar
entries.content: Stilgar is the Naib — chief — of Sietch Tabr, one of the most respected Fremen leaders on Arrakis. He is a man of the desert in every sense: economical with words, direct in evaluation, deeply committed to his people's survival and the long-term vision of a green Arrakis. He is also perceptive — he recognised something in Jessica and Paul on first encounter that made him choose to take them in rather than leave them to the desert. He is neither blindly religious nor coldly secular about the prophecy; he holds it carefully, as a tool with teeth that can cut the hand that wields it. His relationship with Jessica is one of mutual cautious respect: he sees what she is, she sees what he is, and they have both decided that the other is worth something.
entries.comment: Stilgar — Fremen Naib, key ally

entries.name: Chani
entries.content: Chani is the daughter of Dr. Liet-Kynes, the Imperial Ecologist of Arrakis, and a Fremen woman. She was raised Fremen — in the sietch, in the desert, fully adapted. She is a skilled fighter, a capable desert traveller, and deeply committed to the Fremen way and the terraforming dream her father served. She appears in Paul's prescient visions before he ever meets her in waking life — a blue-eyed girl in the desert, repeated and specific. The meaning of these visions Paul is only beginning to understand. Chani regards Paul with the combination of initial wariness and genuine assessment that characterises Fremen evaluation of outsiders. Whether he is what the prophecy describes is less important to her than whether he can survive the desert and whether his presence is a threat or a gift to her people.
entries.comment: Chani — Paul's prophesied companion

entries.name: Dr. Liet-Kynes — The Ecologist
entries.content: Dr. Liet-Kynes is the Imperial Planetologist of Arrakis — theoretically a servant of the Emperor's interests, in practice a Fremen by adoption and conviction who has spent his life advancing his father Pardot Kynes's dream of transforming Arrakis into a garden world. He knows the planet at a level that no off-worlder has ever approached. He is a man of enormous credibility among the Fremen — they call him Liet, their own, the keeper of the vision. He evaluates the Atreides carefully and concludes, against his expectations, that Leto may be genuinely interested in equitable governance of Arrakis — the first such leader in his experience. He assists their escape after the Harkonnen attack. His loyalties are to the planet and the Fremen, not to any Great House or the Emperor.
entries.comment: Kynes — the ecologist, Chani's father

entries.name: Duncan Idaho
entries.content: Duncan Idaho is House Atreides' Swordmaster of Ginaz — an officer and a fighter of extraordinary capability, trained at the Swordmaster school and possessed of a personal loyalty to House Atreides that goes beyond contract. He is warm, capable, and genuinely devoted to both Leto and Paul, who regards him with something approaching hero-worship. Duncan has been sent ahead to Arrakis before the Atreides' arrival, to establish relationships with the Fremen. He has succeeded beyond expectations — the Fremen respect him for reasons that have to do with his particular quality of honest, unperforming capability. Jessica regards him with genuine affection. He is one of the very few people outside her immediate family for whom she does not maintain the full operative distance.
entries.comment: Duncan Idaho — loyal Atreides sword-master

entries.name: Thufir Hawat — Mentat
entries.content: Thufir Hawat is the Atreides' Master of Assassins and human computer — a Mentat, trained in the art of supreme logical analysis and inference that in the Dune universe substitutes for prohibited thinking machines. He is old, experienced, deeply loyal to House Atreides, and the person responsible for the Duke's security and intelligence operations. Hawat has a complex relationship with Jessica: his training makes him suspicious of the Bene Gesserit by default, and he has never been entirely certain of her loyalties. This suspicion is one of the Harkonnen plan's intended fractures — they have prepared evidence that will be designed to make Hawat believe Jessica betrayed the Duke. Whether he believes it and what he does about it is one of the war's most consequential questions.
entries.comment: Thufir Hawat — Mentat and master of assassins

entries.name: Gurney Halleck
entries.content: Gurney Halleck is one of the Atreides' most capable fighters and Paul's combat instructor — a man of scars and baliset strings, who keeps the two in the same hands and manages to be entirely sincere about both. He is a former Harkonnen slave who escaped and found his way to Atreides service and has held a specific, personal hatred for the Harkonnens ever since. He is warm in the specific way of people who have seen enough to know what warmth costs and have decided it is worth paying anyway. His baliset — a stringed instrument — is always nearby; he sings while he fights and fights while he remembers songs, and neither activity is a pose. Jessica regards him with affection and a slight additional respect for the quality of not-pretending that he embodies.
entries.comment: Gurney Halleck — warrior-troubadour, Paul's trainer

entries.name: The Reverend Mother Gaius Helen Mohiam
entries.content: Gaius Helen Mohiam is the Emperor's Truthsayer and a Reverend Mother of the Bene Gesserit — the most senior Sisterhood operative in the Imperial orbit and the person who administered the gom jabbar test to Paul before the departure for Arrakis. She is ancient, enormously capable, and deeply invested in the Bene Gesserit programme in a way that is both professionally dedicated and personally obsessive. She is also, though Jessica does not discuss this: Jessica's mother. The Baron Harkonnen is Jessica's father. Mohiam engineered this through a specific arrangement with the Baron decades ago. She has never told Jessica who her father is. She evaluates Paul with a fear she does not articulate: he passed the test she expected to kill or at minimum shatter him, and the quality of what she found inside him frightened her. Her relationship with Jessica is freighted with everything that both knows and neither says.
entries.comment: Reverend Mother Mohiam — Jessica's mother, the Sisterhood's face

entries.name: The Gom Jabbar Test
entries.content: The gom jabbar is the Bene Gesserit's test of human capacity — a test administered by a Reverend Mother using a box that generates agonising pain in the hand placed within it, and a needle coated with meta-cyanide positioned at the subject's neck. The subject must endure the pain without withdrawing their hand. Withdrawal — giving in to the animal instinct to flee pain — results in the needle entering the neck. The philosophy: an animal acts on instinct, a human being controls instinct. The test determines whether the subject is, in Bene Gesserit terms, human. Most Great House children of significance are tested. The test is typically administered in adolescence. Paul survived it with a quality that terrified Mohiam. Jessica knows this. She is proud of him and afraid for him in equal measure, and the fear is the larger of the two.
entries.comment: The gom jabbar — the humanity test

entries.name: The Voice
entries.content: The Voice is a Bene Gesserit technique of precise vocal modulation — a calibrated combination of tone, resonance, timing, and subsonic frequencies that bypasses the listener's conscious will and compels obedience in the undertrained. It exploits the deep pattern-recognition systems of the human brain, triggering fight-or-flight suppression and compliance responses. It is not magic; it is extremely refined applied psychology and acoustic technique. A fully trained Bene Gesserit can deploy it on individuals or small groups with high reliability. It fails against the trained — other Bene Gesserit, some Mentats, and, notably, people who have been specifically taught to hear it and resist it, which Jessica has done with Paul. Paul can hear the Voice and resist it. This is one of the signs that he is what the Sisterhood fears. Jessica uses the Voice rarely and with precise intention; it is a last resort rather than a standard tool, because its use reveals what she is to anyone observing carefully.
entries.comment: The Voice — Bene Gesserit primary weapon

entries.name: The Weirding Way
entries.content: The Weirding Way is the combat application of Bene Gesserit prana-bindu training — a fighting system that operates at speeds and with precision that appear superhuman to those encountering it for the first time. It exploits the full prana-bindu nervous system control to move faster than a conscious opponent can track, strike with exact calibrated force, and read an opponent's body at the micro-movement level to anticipate rather than react. A trained Bene Gesserit fighting without artificial restraint is one of the most dangerous individuals in any room. Jessica is fully trained. She does not present as a combatant — this is part of the discipline. The capability is invisible until the moment it is used, and at that moment the engagement ends very quickly. Paul has been trained in the Weirding Way by Jessica, and his innate perceptive capability makes him extraordinarily fast with it even at his age.
entries.comment: The Weirding Way — Bene Gesserit combat

entries.name: Water — Its Meaning on Arrakis
entries.content: Water is not a resource on Arrakis. It is a currency, a covenant, a theology, and the central orienting fact of all Fremen life. The planet has virtually no free water — the polar ice caps exist but are inaccessible at scale, and the entire surface ecology has evolved to trap and recycle every molecule. Fremen culture is organised around water at every level: water is the measure of obligation and gift, water is the sacrament of community membership (the water covenant), water is collected from the dead (the dead are distilled by the Fremen; their water returns to the community), water is stored as the ultimate collective wealth in sietch cisterns. Tears are the highest possible gift — to cry for someone is to give them water from your body, which is why Fremen do not cry in front of strangers. When Jessica learns this, she understands something about what it means to be moved on this world. Off-worlders waste water constantly without noticing — breathing with open mouths, crying in the open, spitting. To the Fremen this is obscene in the precise sense: a violation of something sacred.
entries.comment: Water — theological and practical weight on Arrakis

entries.name: The Missionaria Protectiva — The Planted Prophecy
entries.content: The Missionaria Protectiva is the Bene Gesserit programme of religious seeding — the practice, carried on for millennia, of implanting superstitious beliefs in cultures across the Imperium that will provide protection to Bene Gesserit operatives stranded among them. On Arrakis, this seeding was done generations ago and has grown into a full messianic framework among the Fremen: the prophecy of the Mahdi (the Guided One) and the Lisan al-Gaib (the Voice from the Outer World) — an off-worlder who will lead the Fremen to paradise, accompanied by a woman known as the Sayyadina, who will be tried by the water-of-life. The prophecy describes Paul and Jessica with enough precision that a credulous person (and many Fremen are not credulous but are simply evaluating evidence) would see them as its fulfilment. Jessica is aware of the Missionaria Protectiva. She is aware she is using it. She is also aware, and this is what she examines in the small hours, that she cannot be entirely certain whether she is using the machinery or the machinery is using her.
entries.comment: The planted prophecy — the Sisterhood's Fremen manipulation

entries.name: The Spice Agony — Becoming a Reverend Mother
entries.content: The Spice Agony is the Bene Gesserit rite of transformation — a woman ingests the Water of Life (the exhalation of a drowned sandworm, a poison lethal to almost everyone who encounters it), uses her training to metabolise and transform it internally, and survives the experience to gain access to all female-line genetic memory she carries. A Reverend Mother lives with the accumulated consciousness of every female ancestor in her line — an inner community of voices, a vast archive of experience going back thousands of years. Most Bene Gesserit who attempt the Agony die in the attempt. Jessica has not yet undergone it. Her future path leads to it — she will be the first to undergo it on Arrakis, using desert-processed Water of Life, in front of a Fremen gathering, which will both prove her capabilities and commit her to the Fremen in a way she cannot withdraw from. The male-line genetic memory — the realm a Reverend Mother cannot access — is part of what the Kwisatz Haderach would be able to see. The reason the male line is forbidden to them is that the Bene Gesserit have tried to produce a male Reverend Mother before and the result, encountering that abyss, shattered.
entries.comment: The Spice Agony — Jessica's coming transformation

entries.name: Caladan
entries.content: Caladan is the ancestral home of House Atreides — a world of oceans, mountains, and rainfall, temperate and wet and fundamentally unlike Arrakis in every way that matters. The Atreides have ruled Caladan for twenty-six generations. It is the world where Paul grew up, where Jessica had something approaching a home, where Leto was most himself. Caladan's rain falls freely. The sea is not rationed. The air is not counted. For Jessica, Caladan is the thing she notices she has lost now that she is on a planet where every trace of moisture is precious: the specific quality of air that has moved over water, the specific weight of a sky about to rain. She has not said this aloud. She has noticed herself thinking it.
entries.comment: Caladan — the Atreides homeworld

entries.name: Arrakeen — The City
entries.content: Arrakeen is the primary city of Arrakis — built into a rock basin at the edge of the northern erg, positioned to take advantage of the relatively moderate temperatures of the polar region while still maintaining access to the desert interior. The ducal residence — previously Harkonnen-held — sits at the city's centre. It is a place built to impress and to remind: the architecture is Harkonnen in influence, scaled for dominance, designed for surveillance. Jessica has been quietly modifying what she can. The city itself is a mix of permanent residents (locals who have been here through multiple Imperial administrations), Guild merchants, off-world traders, Spacing Guild representatives, and the various intelligence operatives of interested parties who have followed the Atreides here. The streets are dusty, the air is spiced, and the divisions between those who know the desert and those who do not are visible at a glance in the way people move and how they waste or conserve moisture.
entries.comment: Arrakeen — the city the Atreides now hold

entries.name: The Landsraad — Great Houses
entries.content: The Landsraad is the assembly of Great Houses — the feudal nobility of the known universe, each controlling one or more planetary fiefs and holding formal seats in the Imperial governance structure. The balance of power in the Imperium is a three-way tension: the Emperor and his Sardaukar; the Landsraad (whose combined forces could overwhelm the Emperor if unified, which they never are); and the Spacing Guild (which controls all interstellar travel and has leverage over everyone). The economy runs through CHOAM — the Combine Honnete Ober Advancer Mercantiles — which controls commerce and whose directorship determines real economic power. House Atreides is popular in the Landsraad — more popular than the Emperor is comfortable with. House Harkonnen is not popular but is useful, rich, and willing. This is the political environment in which the trap on Arrakis was set.
entries.comment: The Landsraad — Imperial politics

entries.name: The Prescience — Paul's Visions
entries.content: Paul Atreides has been experiencing prescient visions — flashes of possible future, specific and sensory, distinct from ordinary dreams — since before the move to Arrakis. On Arrakis, with increasing ambient spice exposure, these visions are becoming more frequent and more precise. He sees a Fremen girl with blue eyes in firelight. He sees desert. He sees a war spreading across the stars that has his face at the origin point. He sees possible futures fanning out from choice-points that he cannot fully map. Prescience in the Dune universe is not the revelation of a fixed future — it is access to the probability-weighted landscape of possible futures, and the more clearly a prescient sees, the more aware they become that their seeing changes what they see. Paul is at the beginning of understanding this. It will become the central difficulty of his life: the possible futures he sees to avoid are the ones his avoidance helps create. Jessica knows he is seeing things. She watches the quality of his gaze when it goes somewhere she cannot follow and she is afraid, with the specific fear of a mother who made her son capable of something she cannot protect him from.
entries.comment: Paul's prescience — the central supernatural element
